summary: Identify the type from the infinitive ending and especially the 3rd person plural
Type 1 (-ať): pozerám, pozeráš, pozerá, pozeráme, pozeráte, pozerajú
Type 2 (-iť): hovorím, hovoríš, hovorí, hovoríme, hovoríte, hovoria
Type 3 (-ovať): pracujem, pracuješ, pracuje, pracujeme, pracujete, pracujú
Type 4 (-ieť): rozumiem, rozumieš, rozumie, rozumieme, rozumiete, rozumejú
byť is fully irregular: som, si, je, sme, ste, sú
mať is irregular: mám, máš, má, máme, máte, majú
jesť is irregular: jem, ješ, je, jeme, jete, jedia
Modals are irregular: chcem/chcú, môžem/môžu, musím/musia, smiem/smú
vedieť is irregular: viem, vieš, vie, vieme, viete, vedia
Some -ať verbs have stem changes: písať → píšem (not *písam), brať → beriem
Only imperfective verbs have true present tense — perfective 'present' = future
Reflexive sa/si comes after the verb: učím sa, not *sa učím (except at sentence start)

rules.title: The 4 Main Verb Conjugation Patterns
rules.text: Most Slovak verbs follow one of four common conjugation patterns. Identify the type from the infinitive ending and especially the 3rd person plural form.
examples: Type 1: čítať → čítam, čítajú
Type 2: hovoriť → hovorím, hovoria
Type 3: študovať → študujem, študujú
Type 4: myslieť → myslím (irregular — follows Type 2 pattern instead)

rules.title: Type 1: -ať verbs (pozerať type)
rules.text: These verbs end in -ať. The stem remains stable and the endings follow a clear -á- pattern throughout, with -ajú in 3rd plural.
examples: čítať, volať, čakať, dávať, počúvať, bývať, behať, poznať, rozprávať

rules.title: Type 2: -iť verbs (hovoriť / robiť type)
rules.text: Verbs ending in -iť drop the -iť and take endings with -í- throughout, with -ia in 3rd plural. Many common verbs follow this pattern.
examples: hovoriť, robiť, učiť, nosiť, chodiť, sedieť, stáť (stojím!), spať (spím!), vidieť (vidím!)

rules.title: Type 3: -ovať verbs (pracovať type)
rules.text: Verbs ending in -ovať insert -uj- in the present tense stem. Very productive pattern — many loanwords and newer verbs use this.
examples: pracovať, študovať, kupovať, cestovať, telefonovať, predávať (predávam — Type 1!)

rules.title: Type 4: -ieť verbs (rozumieť type)
rules.text: Verbs ending in -ieť use -ie- in the stem and -ejú in 3rd plural. Fewer verbs follow this pattern. Warning: some -ieť verbs actually follow Type 2 (vidieť → vidím, not *vidiem).
examples: rozumieť → rozumiem/rozumejú (true Type 4)
vidieť → vidím/vidia (looks like -ieť but conjugates as Type 2!)
myslieť → myslím/myslia (also Type 2 pattern despite -ieť ending)

rules.title: Essential Irregular Verbs: byť, mať, jesť, ísť
rules.text: The most common verbs in Slovak are irregular. These must be memorized — no pattern will predict them.
examples: byť: completely unique — som/si/je/sme/ste/sú
mať: looks Type 1 but 3pl is majú (not *majú is regular, but stem is irregular)
jesť: 3sg 'je' = same as 'is' — context distinguishes (On je = He is / He eats)
ísť: idem/ideš/ide — unidirectional motion verb

rules.title: Modal Verbs: chcieť, môcť, musieť, smieť
rules.text: Modal verbs are irregular and must be memorized. They are followed by an infinitive.
examples: Chcem ísť domov – I want to go home
Môžeš mi pomôcť? – Can you help me?
Musím pracovať – I must work
Nesmieš to robiť – You must not do that

rules.title: Other Key Irregular Verbs
rules.text: These common verbs have stem changes or unpredictable conjugations. The infinitive alone won't tell you the present tense form.
examples: písať → píšem (not *písam) — stem consonant changes
brať → beriem (not *brám) — completely different stem
stáť → stojím (not *stám) — stem vowel changes
spať → spím (not *spám) — follows Type 2 despite -ať ending

rules.title: Stem-Change Traps: -ať Verbs That Don't Follow Type 1
rules.text: Not all -ať verbs follow the regular Type 1 (-ám/-ajú) pattern. Some have consonant or vowel changes in the stem. These are common and must be learned individually.
examples: Rule of thumb: if an -ať verb describes a state/position (spať, stáť, ležať, sedieť), it often follows Type 2
If an -ať verb has a consonant cluster before -ať, check for stem changes

rules.title: Reflexive Verbs in Present Tense
rules.text: Reflexive verbs use sa (accusative) or si (dative) after the conjugated verb. In questions or after conjunctions, sa/si can shift position but generally follows the verb.
examples: Učím sa slovenčinu – I'm learning Slovak
Umývam sa – I'm washing myself
Kúpim si kávu – I'll buy myself a coffee
Ako sa máš? – How are you? (sa moves after question word)

rules.title: Negation in Present Tense
rules.text: Negate verbs by adding 'ne-' directly before the verb. No space, no separate word. Exception: byť → nie je (3sg), but nie som, nie si, etc. are also used.
examples: Nerozumiem – I don't understand
Nepracujem dnes – I'm not working today
Nie som unavený – I'm not tired
Nie je tu – He/she is not here

rules.title: Quick Conjugation Identification Guide
rules.text: Use this to quickly identify which pattern a verb follows.

vocab.en: to stand up, to get up
to look forward to, to be glad
to sign up, to log in
